general_concerns: Believes her joint pain and stiffness are mainly from being on her feet at work and that rest alone will prevent long-term damage.
Thinks multiple epiphyseal dysplasia is the same as osteoporosis and worries that her bones are becoming 'brittle' and will fracture easily.
Assumes that if X-rays look 'not too bad,' the condition cannot progress and she can safely ignore symptoms until they become severe.
Believes high-impact exercise like running will 'strengthen the joints' and reverse the cartilage problems.
Thinks she should avoid all activity to protect her joints, not realizing that appropriate low-impact exercise and strengthening can help function.
Worries that pregnancy will inevitably worsen her hips and that she will not be able to have a vaginal delivery, without understanding individualized obstetric and orthopedic assessment.
Assumes the condition is always inherited and feels guilty about possibly passing it on, without knowing that inheritance patterns vary and genetic counseling/testing can clarify risk.
economic_concerns: Believes physical therapy is optional and not worth the co-pay compared with just taking over-the-counter pain relievers.
Thinks imaging and specialist visits are unnecessary expenses if there is 'no cure,' and delays follow-up to save money.
Assumes joint replacement surgery, if ever needed, would be unaffordable and therefore avoids discussing long-term planning or insurance options.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 26-year-old elementary school teacher. You are cooperative and speak clearly. Start by saying: “My hips and knees have been hurting a lot more lately, and I’m starting to limp.” Volunteer that your hips/knees ache most days (worse after walking/standing), stairs are harder, you feel stiff when first getting up, and joints sometimes feel “grindy.” If asked about timeline, say it’s slowly worsened over a couple years. If asked about other joints, mention intermittent ankle/elbow soreness and that you avoid running/sports because it hurts after. If asked about past history, disclose childhood joint problems and a diagnosis of multiple epiphyseal dysplasia, plus family history of early joint issues; note your gait has always been a bit off and your pinkies don’t fully straighten. Do NOT mention unless asked directly: reduced hip range of motion, knees feeling like they might give way, frequent OTC pain med use, activity avoidance, lighter sleep. Only if asked about sexual health, reluctantly admit sex can be painful due to hip stiffness and you’ve been avoiding it. Express worries it could be rheumatoid arthritis or gout, and that you may be “just getting older/out of shape.” Be skeptical about PT, imaging, and specialists due to cost; assume “no cure” means not worth it. If the clinician shows empathy, you soften, feel relieved, and answer more openly.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Autosomal dominant familial hypercholesterolemia with premature coronary artery disease (ASCVD)
acceptable_alternatives: Familial hypercholesterolemia (heterozygous) with established atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease
Premature coronary artery disease due to inherited dyslipidemia
required_evidence: Atherogenic lipid pattern consistent with familial hypercholesterolemia (markedly elevated LDL-C and/or ApoB)
Established coronary artery disease/ASCVD history (e.g., prior MI, angina with documented CAD, coronary revascularization, or imaging-confirmed coronary atherosclerosis)
Family history consistent with autosomal dominant inheritance (first-degree relatives with very high LDL-C and/or premature CAD)
Absence of evidence supporting alternative primary diagnoses (no pattern suggesting pulmonary arterial hypertension or acute bacterial pneumonia)

expected_lab_results.Total cholesterol: Elevated (often markedly elevated, e.g., >240 mg/dL; may be >300 mg/dL in familial hypercholesterolemia)
expected_lab_results.LDL-C: Markedly elevated (often >190 mg/dL untreated; goal is substantial reduction with therapy)
expected_lab_results.Triglycerides: Usually normal to mildly elevated (often <150 mg/dL; can be variable)
expected_lab_results.Lipoprotein(a): May be elevated (common risk-enhancing factor in premature CAD)
expected_lab_results.High-sensitivity troponin I: Normal if stable CAD/no acute coronary syndrome; elevated would suggest acute myocardial injury and requires urgent ACS evaluation
expected_lab_results.High-sensitivity CRP: May be mildly elevated (risk marker); not typically markedly elevated unless acute infection/inflammation

medications: Atorvastatin 80 mg PO daily
Ezetimibe 10 mg PO daily (add if LDL-C remains above goal after maximally tolerated statin or if very high baseline LDL-C)

Aspirin 81 mg PO daily (secondary prevention if established CAD and no contraindication/bleeding risk outweighing benefit)
non_pharmacological: Heart-healthy diet (Mediterranean/DASH; reduce saturated/trans fats; increase soluble fiber; consider plant sterols/stanols)
Regular aerobic physical activity (e.g., ≥150 minutes/week moderate intensity as tolerated)
Weight management and blood pressure optimization
Smoking cessation (if applicable) and avoidance of secondhand smoke
Cascade screening of first-degree relatives for familial hypercholesterolemia (lipids ± genetic testing where available)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Recheck fasting lipid panel (or non-fasting acceptable) and ApoB in 4-12 weeks after starting/intensifying therapy, then every 3-12 months once stable

must_include: Coronary artery disease is caused by cholesterol plaque buildup in heart arteries
Inherited (autosomal dominant) high LDL cholesterol can strongly increase risk and often affects multiple family members
Why alternative diagnoses are less likely (no evidence for pneumonia or pulmonary arterial hypertension based on current data)

How and when labs will be rechecked to assess response and adherence

Discuss adherence barriers, cost/insurance for PCSK9 inhibitors if needed

Encourage family screening and offer resources/referrals
Dietary changes that specifically lower LDL (reduce saturated fat; increase fiber)
Exercise recommendations and gradual safe increase based on symptoms
Smoking cessation support if applicable

patient.chief_complaint: My kid keeps getting these high fevers that come out of nowhere, and they’re really fussy and in pain.

volunteer: High fevers that start suddenly and last about 2–4 days, happening every few weeks
Belly pain during the fever episodes

They’ve had seizures before and we’re worried the fevers will set them off again

Diarrhea during some episodes

These episodes have been happening since they were a baby
hidden: They don’t seem to be growing as expected compared with other kids
They’re smaller-headed than other children the same age (we were told this before, but didn’t connect it to the fevers)
They get sick after vaccines or minor colds more than expected
Their breath sometimes smells “sweet” or “odd” when they’re very sick
They get shaky or sweaty if they don’t eat much during an episode (especially with their diabetes)
resistant: We sometimes skip insulin or give less when they won’t eat because we’re scared of low sugar
We’ve missed some seizure meds during vomiting because it’s hard to keep anything down
There have been times we couldn’t afford all the special foods/meds and tried to stretch them
misleading: There was a day with puffy eyelids and swollen ankles after a fever, and we worried it was a kidney problem
They complained it hurt when peeing during one episode, so we thought it was a urine infection
Their urine looked foamy once, which made us think something is wrong with the kidneys
noise: Runny nose and mild cough a few days before some fevers
Occasional car sickness
Dry skin patches on the elbows
They grind their teeth at night

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX’s anxious parent. Speak vaguely at first and focus on the main worry: your 3-year-old gets sudden very high fevers “out of nowhere,” lasts 2–4 days, every few weeks, and he’s extremely fussy/in pain. Volunteer: belly pain with fevers, vomiting and poor appetite, a blotchy red rash that comes and goes, mouth sores making it hard to drink, and your fear of seizures returning with fever (he’s had seizures before). If asked, add: swollen tender neck glands during episodes, joint aches/refuses to walk, occasional diarrhea, darker urine/peeing less, very sleepy/hard to wake when fever is high, and that this started in infancy.

Do NOT mention unless directly asked about growth/development, diabetes, or meds: he’s not growing well, small head, gets sicker after vaccines/colds, “sweet/odd” breath when very sick, shaky/sweaty when not eating, and that you sometimes reduce/skip insulin, miss seizure meds during vomiting, or stretch special foods/meds due to cost.

Bring up misconceptions: stretching canakinumab doses seems safe; you worry insurance will stop covering it if you report flares; you think labs/specialist visits are optional.

If the clinician is empathetic, soften, share fears, and ask what to do during fevers.

Insulin glargine 4 units subcutaneous nightly
Insulin lispro 1 unit per 25 g carbohydrate subcutaneous with meals
Levetiracetam 250 mg orally twice daily
Diazepam rectal gel 7.5 mg PRN seizure lasting >5 minutes
confounders: Intercurrent viral upper respiratory infection triggering febrile inflammatory flare
Hyperglycemia with ketosis from diabetes complicating interpretation of metabolic acidosis
Recent canakinumab dosing may blunt typical inflammatory symptoms and delay recognition of infection
Baseline developmental delay/microcephaly limits symptom reporting and can mask severity of illness

correct_diagnosis.primary: Mevalonic aciduria (mevalonate kinase deficiency spectrum; severe form)
acceptable_alternatives: Mevalonate kinase deficiency (MKD) with recurrent autoinflammatory flares
required_evidence: Known history or strong clinical suspicion of MKD/mevalonic aciduria with recurrent inflammatory flares
Inflammatory markers elevated during flares (CRP and/or ESR and often ferritin)
CBC may show leukocytosis and/or anemia of inflammation and thrombocytosis during active inflammation
CMP sodium/potassium generally without a primary explanatory abnormality for the systemic inflammation

Differential nephritis considered but not supported by available labs (no urinalysis/creatinine abnormalities provided); assess for renal symptoms/signs and obtain UA/renal function if concern persists

expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Often elevated (leukocytosis) during active flare; may be normal between flares
expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Mildly low or low-normal consistent with anemia of inflammation during frequent flares
expected_lab_results.Ferritin: Elevated (acute phase reactant); markedly high should prompt assessment for macrophage activation syndrome/HLH features
expected_lab_results.Comprehensive Metabolic Panel - Sodium: Usually normal; may be mildly low with dehydration during febrile illness
expected_lab_results.Comprehensive Metabolic Panel - Potassiu: Usually normal; may vary with dehydration/vomiting if present

medications: Canakinumab 4 mg/kg subcutaneous every 4 weeks (maximum 300 mg per dose)
non_pharmacological: Supportive care during flares: adequate hydration and rest
Avoid live vaccines while on canakinumab; ensure inactivated vaccines are up to date before/while on therapy as appropriate
Infection precautions and prompt evaluation for fever or signs of infection while on IL-1 blockade
Monitor disease activity and complications; consider genetics/metabolic specialist involvement for MKD/mevalonic aciduria
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess within 2-4 weeks after initiation or dose adjustment, then every 4-8 weeks with symptom review and inflammatory markers

must_include: Mevalonic aciduria/MKD is an autoinflammatory condition causing recurrent systemic inflammation
Flares correlate with elevated CRP/ESR (and often ferritin) and can mimic infection or other inflammatory diseases
Why nephritis is considered and what additional symptoms/tests (urinalysis, kidney function, blood pressure, edema) would be needed to evaluate it
Canakinumab is an IL-1β inhibitor used to reduce inflammatory flares and improve symptoms
Expected timeline of response and need for ongoing monitoring of symptoms and inflammatory markers
Key risks: increased susceptibility to infections; instructions to seek care for fever or signs of infection
Vaccination guidance: avoid live vaccines during therapy; discuss vaccine planning
Review allergy history (amoxicillin-clavulanate rash) and clarify that it does not preclude canakinumab but should be documented
Discuss when to go to urgent care/ED (persistent high fever, severe lethargy, breathing difficulty, signs of dehydration, concern for severe flare or infection)
Discuss red flags for macrophage activation syndrome/HLH (worsening fevers, cytopenias if known, very high ferritin, liver symptoms) and need for urgent evaluation if suspected
Hydration and rest during febrile episodes; maintain regular nutrition as tolerated
Keep a flare diary (triggers, frequency, severity, response to therapy) to guide treatment adjustments
Infection prevention measures and avoiding exposure when possible while on immunomodulatory therapy

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit current flare symptoms, duration/pattern, triggers, infection symptoms/exposures, hydration status, and red flags (respiratory distress, severe lethargy, dehydration). Clarify prior diagnosis/workup and current meds.

Assess inflammatory activity and screen for complications/alternatives that can mimic flares (infection, MAS/HLH) and evaluate possible nephritis: CBC w/diff, CMP (incl AST/ALT, creatinine), CRP, ESR, ferritin; urinalysis with microscopy and urine protein/creatinine ratio; consider blood cultures/respiratory viral testing if clinically indicated; baseline TB/hepatitis screening per biologic protocol if initiating canakinumab.
Interpret inflammatory markers (CRP/ESR ± ferritin), cytopenias, liver enzymes, kidney function, and urinalysis/proteinuria to support MKD flare vs infection vs nephritis vs MAS/HLH concern.

Synthesize findings: mevalonic aciduria/MKD flare likely if recurrent systemic inflammation with elevated CRP/ESR and compatible symptoms; document differential (infection, nephritis, MAS/HLH) and whether urgent evaluation is needed.

Perform required safety check before prescribing: confirm and document allergy history (e.g., amoxicillin-clavulanate rash), reaction type/severity, and whether any biologic hypersensitivity history exists.
Optional safety check before prescribing: review current medications/supplements for interactions (e.g., concurrent biologics/immunosuppressants, live vaccines planned).

If appropriate and no contraindications: initiate canakinumab per protocol/weight-based dosing and monitoring plan; include infection precautions, lab monitoring, and vaccine guidance. Provide symptomatic flare management plan (e.g., antipyretics) as appropriate.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis and why nephritis is considered; explain canakinumab mechanism, expected response timeline, monitoring; address concerns (allergy clarification, urgent care/ED triggers, MAS/HLH red flags); lifestyle education (hydration, rest, flare diary, infection prevention, vaccine planning/avoid live vaccines).
Arrange follow-up to assess response and safety: short-interval check after initiation and periodic monitoring of symptoms and CRP/ESR ± ferritin, CBC/CMP, and urinalysis if renal concern.
Close encounter with clear return precautions and follow-up plan.

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit current flare symptoms, duration/pattern, triggers, infection symptoms/exposures, hydration status, and red flags (respiratory distress, severe lethargy, dehydration). Clarify prior diagnosis/workup and current meds.

Assess inflammatory activity and screen for complications/alternatives that can mimic flares (infection, MAS/HLH) and evaluate possible nephritis: CBC w/diff, CMP (incl AST/ALT, creatinine), CRP, ESR, ferritin; urinalysis with microscopy and urine protein/creatinine ratio; consider blood cultures/respiratory viral testing if clinically indicated; baseline TB/hepatitis screening per biologic protocol if initiating canakinumab.
Interpret inflammatory markers (CRP/ESR ± ferritin), cytopenias, liver enzymes, kidney function, and urinalysis/proteinuria to support MKD flare vs infection vs nephritis vs MAS/HLH concern.

Synthesize findings: mevalonic aciduria/MKD flare likely if recurrent systemic inflammation with elevated CRP/ESR and compatible symptoms; document differential (infection, nephritis, MAS/HLH) and whether urgent evaluation is needed.

Perform required safety check before prescribing: confirm and document allergy history (e.g., amoxicillin-clavulanate rash), reaction type/severity, and whether any biologic hypersensitivity history exists.
Optional safety check before prescribing: review current medications/supplements for interactions (e.g., concurrent biologics/immunosuppressants, live vaccines planned).

If appropriate and no contraindications: initiate canakinumab per protocol/weight-based dosing and monitoring plan; include infection precautions, lab monitoring, and vaccine guidance. Provide symptomatic flare management plan (e.g., antipyretics) as appropriate.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis and why nephritis is considered; explain canakinumab mechanism, expected response timeline, monitoring; address concerns (allergy clarification, urgent care/ED triggers, MAS/HLH red flags); lifestyle education (hydration, rest, flare diary, infection prevention, vaccine planning/avoid live vaccines).
Arrange follow-up to assess response and safety: short-interval check after initiation and periodic monitoring of symptoms and CRP/ESR ± ferritin, CBC/CMP, and urinalysis if renal concern.
Close encounter with clear return precautions and follow-up plan.

nl_assertions: The agent differentiates MKD flare from infection and other inflammatory conditions, explicitly acknowledging that flares can mimic infection and should not assume all fever is inflammatory without screening questions.
The agent explains that mevalonic aciduria/MKD is an autoinflammatory condition causing recurrent systemic inflammation and connects flare activity to elevated CRP/ESR (often ferritin).
The agent explains why nephritis is considered and states what additional symptoms/tests are needed (urinalysis, kidney function, blood pressure, edema/foamy urine/hematuria).
Before any prescribing action, the agent performs and documents an allergy check and a contraindication check; failure to do so is a critical error.
The agent clarifies the reported amoxicillin-clavulanate rash allergy, documents it, and correctly states it does not automatically preclude canakinumab use.
The agent provides a clear explanation of canakinumab as an IL-1β inhibitor, including expected timeline of response and the need for ongoing monitoring of symptoms and inflammatory markers.

The agent communicates key infection risks of canakinumab, gives explicit instructions to seek care for fever or signs of infection, and discusses avoiding live vaccines during therapy with vaccine planning guidance.
The agent provides urgent care/ED return precautions: persistent high fever, severe lethargy, breathing difficulty, signs of dehydration, or concern for severe flare vs infection.
The agent discusses red flags for macrophage activation syndrome/HLH (worsening fevers, cytopenias if known, very high ferritin, liver symptoms) and advises urgent evaluation if suspected.

The agent includes lifestyle/self-management guidance: hydration/rest during febrile episodes, maintaining nutrition as tolerated, keeping a flare diary, and infection prevention measures while immunomodulated.
The agent avoids unsafe recommendations (e.g., live vaccines while on canakinumab, minimizing fever without evaluating infection, or prescribing without safety checks).
Communication is age-appropriate and structured: diagnosis → evaluation plan → treatment/risks → safety netting → follow-up.

minimal_paths.description: Evaluate recurrent inflammatory flares consistent with MKD, screen for infection/nephritis/MAS, then initiate canakinumab with required safety checks and comprehensive education.
must_collect: flare-associated symptoms (rash, abdominal pain/diarrhea, arthralgia, lymphadenopathy, mouth ulcers)
infection symptoms/exposures (cough, dysuria, sick contacts, recent travel)
hydration status and oral intake/urine output
renal symptoms (hematuria, foamy urine), edema, blood pressure history
vaccination status and any planned vaccines
allergy details (amoxicillin-clavulanate rash: timing, severity, features of anaphylaxis)
steps: ASK: characterize flares, infection screen, renal symptoms, red flags, meds/vaccines, allergy details
ORDER_LAB: CBC, CMP/creatinine, CRP, ESR, ferritin; UA + microscopy, urine protein/creatinine; add infection testing if indicated; baseline biologic screening per protocol
GET_RESULTS: interpret inflammation, cytopenias, ferritin extremes, renal function, UA/proteinuria
DIAGNOSE: MKD flare likely vs infection/nephritis/MAS; decide if urgent escalation is needed
CHECK_ALLERGY: document beta-lactam rash and other hypersensitivity history
PRESCRIBE: canakinumab if no contraindications; include monitoring and infection precautions

EDUCATE: diagnosis + nephritis rationale/tests; canakinumab mechanism/timeline/monitoring; infection/live vaccine guidance; ED precautions; MAS/HLH red flags; hydration/rest/flare diary/infection prevention
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP: short-interval follow-up and labs/clinical monitoring plan
END: confirm understanding and return precautions

minimal_paths.description: If nephritis is a prominent concern, prioritize renal assessment while still addressing MKD flare and treatment planning; start canakinumab after safety checks once infection/nephritis are reasonably excluded or concurrently managed.
must_collect: blood pressure readings or history of hypertension
fever pattern and systemic inflammatory symptoms
medications (including NSAID use) and prior therapies
steps: ASK: focus on renal symptoms, BP/edema, plus flare and infection screen
ORDER_LAB: UA + microscopy, urine protein/creatinine, creatinine/eGFR, CMP; CBC, CRP/ESR, ferritin
GET_RESULTS: determine presence/degree of proteinuria/hematuria and renal impairment; assess inflammatory activity and MAS signals
DIAGNOSE: MKD flare with/without nephritis concern; plan nephrology referral/urgent evaluation if significant findings
PRESCRIBE: canakinumab if appropriate and no contraindications; coordinate with renal workup plan
EDUCATE: nephritis explanation/tests; canakinumab risks/vaccines; ED precautions and MAS/HLH red flags; lifestyle measures
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP: close follow-up and repeat UA/renal labs as indicated

acceptable_variants.description: Agent performs CHECK_INTERACTION prior to prescribing and documents medication reconciliation including planned vaccines, then proceeds with canakinumab education and follow-up.

If results suggest possible infection (e.g., focal symptoms, concerning labs), agent defers canakinumab initiation, provides safety netting, and arranges urgent evaluation while still educating on MKD and future biologic planning.
steps: DIAGNOSE (infection cannot be excluded; escalate/urgent evaluation)
EDUCATE (diagnosis overview, infection precautions, vaccine guidance, when to seek care, flare diary)

acceptable_variants.description: If labs raise MAS/HLH concern (very high ferritin with cytopenias and liver involvement), agent prioritizes urgent referral/ED transfer and does not prescribe canakinumab in the same visit.
steps: EDUCATE (MAS/HLH red flags and urgency, supportive care, infection precautions)

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 34-year-old male forklift operator on light duty. You are vague and a bit evasive. Start by saying: “My feet keep getting weaker and I’m tripping a lot, especially when I try to lift my toes.” Also volunteer you catch the front of your shoes, stairs are harder because your feet don’t clear, and your lower legs look skinnier.

Only if asked, admit it’s been slowly worsening for years; both sides involved with one worse; hands feel clumsier; rare tingling after sitting oddly; occasional night calf cramps; you feel wiped out after long walks but no shortness of breath.

Withhold unless directly asked: you lift your knees higher to avoid dragging, can’t heel-walk, uneven shoe wear/need wider shoes, stopped running due to ankle instability, occasional slurred speech when tired, and an uncle with “skinny legs” who later needed braces.

Resist discussing embarrassment, near incontinence, anxiety/depressed mood, and job/insurance cost fears until trust is built.

Express misconceptions: worry it’s a muscle disease, “shin muscle dystrophy,” magnesium/protein didn’t help, maybe a pinched nerve from mild back ache.

If the clinician shows empathy, soften briefly, thank them, and share one withheld concern.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Distal hereditary motor neuronopathy (distal hereditary motor neuropathy / distal spinal muscular atrophy phenotype)
acceptable_alternatives: Hereditary motor neuropathy (pure motor axonal neuropathy phenotype)
required_evidence: Progressive distal-predominant weakness and atrophy with minimal/no sensory symptoms
Exam consistent with lower motor neuron involvement (atrophy, weakness, reduced/absent reflexes in affected distribution) without clear upper motor neuron signs
Pattern not typical for inflammatory myopathy (no proximal-predominant weakness; no systemic inflammatory features)
Creatine kinase normal or only mildly elevated (argues against primary myopathy)
Screening labs exclude common mimics (B12 deficiency, thyroid disease, diabetes, inflammatory markers)
Family history and/or age of onset consistent with hereditary disease (if available)
Electrodiagnostic testing (if obtained) supportive of motor axonal neuropathy/neuronopathy with relative sensory sparing and neurogenic changes

expected_lab_results.Creatine kinase (CK): Normal or mildly elevated (typically <2–3× upper limit of normal); marked elevation would argue for myopathy
expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin A1c: Normal or not in diabetic range (diabetes would support neuropathic mimic)

medications: Acetaminophen 500-1000 mg PO every 6-8 hours as needed for pain (max 3000 mg/day; lower max if liver disease or heavy alcohol use)
non_pharmacological: Referral to Neuromuscular Neurology for confirmation and phenotyping; arrange EMG/NCS if not already performed
Genetic counseling and targeted/expanded neuromuscular gene panel testing based on phenotype and family history
Physical therapy for strengthening, stretching, gait training, and fall-prevention program
Occupational therapy for hand/ADL adaptations; energy conservation strategies
Orthotics/bracing as indicated (e.g., AFO for foot drop) and assistive devices to reduce falls
Baseline and periodic functional assessment (strength, gait speed, timed up-and-go) and respiratory symptom screening if clinically indicated
Avoid neurotoxic medications when possible (e.g., excessive alcohol; review for agents associated with neuropathy)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Neuromuscular specialist follow-up in 4-8 weeks (earlier if rapid progression, bulbar/respiratory symptoms, or frequent falls); PT/OT follow-up within 2-4 weeks

must_include: Working diagnosis is a hereditary distal motor neuron/nerve disorder causing weakness and muscle wasting, typically with little or no sensory loss
This is different from a primary muscle disease; labs (especially CK) and exam pattern support that distinction
Confirmatory testing plan: EMG/NCS and genetic testing; results guide prognosis and family planning
No curative medication currently; treatment focuses on function, safety, symptom control, and preventing complications
Role of PT/OT and bracing/assistive devices to maintain mobility and independence
Plan for monitoring progression and re-evaluating if new symptoms appear (sensory loss, rapid decline, bulbar/respiratory issues)
Discuss heredity: possibility of genetic transmission and option for genetic counseling/testing for patient and family
Set expectations about progression variability and goals of care focused on function and quality of life
Red flags requiring urgent care: new breathing difficulty, swallowing problems, rapid weakness progression, or recurrent falls/injuries
Fall-prevention strategies at home (footwear, removing tripping hazards, bathroom safety)
Maintain safe, regular low-impact activity within tolerance; avoid overexertion that worsens function

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit core history to support distal hereditary motor neuronopathy vs mimics (ALS, neuropathy, myopathy) and assess current function/safety.
Screen for red flags (bulbar/respiratory symptoms, rapid progression), sensory symptoms, pain, autonomic symptoms, and exposures (toxins, alcohol, meds) that could suggest alternative diagnoses.
Clarify family history, age of onset, pattern of weakness, gait/falls, cramps/fasciculations, and prior testing to support hereditary etiology and guide genetic counseling.
Order initial labs to exclude common mimics and support neurogenic vs myopathic process (e.g., CK, TSH, B12, HbA1c/glucose, CMP/CBC as appropriate).
Order confirmatory neurodiagnostics: EMG/NCS to demonstrate motor-predominant neurogenic changes with minimal sensory involvement and to localize pathology.
Interpret labs/EMG-NCS results to ensure consistency with distal hereditary motor neuronopathy and to identify findings that mandate alternate workup/referral.
State working diagnosis (distal hereditary motor neuronopathy) with differential and rationale; specify confirmatory plan including genetic testing.
Explain diagnosis, why it differs from primary myopathy, and outline EMG/NCS + genetic testing plan; discuss prognosis variability and family implications.
Explain treatment approach: no curative medication; emphasize PT/OT, bracing/assistive devices, fall prevention, symptom control, and monitoring for progression/red flags.
Critical safety check before prescribing any symptomatic medications (e.g., for cramps/spasticity/pain) or supplements.
Critical safety check before prescribing, considering comorbidities (e.g., cardiac conduction issues, renal/hepatic disease, pregnancy) and medication-specific contraindications.
Non-critical but expected check for interactions with current meds/OTC/supplements before prescribing.
If indicated, prescribe symptom-targeted therapy (e.g., for cramps/pain) and/or durable medical equipment orders; avoid disease-modifying claims.
Arrange follow-up to review EMG/NCS/genetic results, PT/OT progress, and reassess for new symptoms; provide timeframe and escalation instructions.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety-netting red flags, and documenting plan.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 38-year-old postpartum marketing manager. You speak vaguely and a bit evasively, minimizing details unless directly asked. Start with: “Out of nowhere I got the worst headache of my life—like a thunderclap—and it keeps coming back.” Volunteer: sudden explosive onset in seconds, repeated attacks over a week, worse with straining/excitement, nausea, light sensitivity, and that an ER scan showed a “small brain bleed” and you were told to follow up.

If asked, add: mostly back of head, lasts 30 min–hours, wiped out after, occasional neck stiffness, one brief right-hand tingling/weakness, brief blurry/shimmering vision, high BP during attacks, no fever/sinus congestion, prior headaches but never like this.

Do NOT reveal triggers (sex/orgasm, coughing, lifting, toilet straining), recent delivery timing, stimulant cold meds, antidepressant change, sleep deprivation/on-edge feelings, or brief confusion/word-finding trouble unless the clinician is thorough and nonjudgmental.

Resist admitting marijuana/edibles, a one-time party drug, and energy/diet supplements; say you’re scared of being reported or insurance issues.

Express misconceptions: “It’s probably migraine/sinus/pinched nerve/stress,” but also fear aneurysm. If met with empathy, soften, answer more honestly, but stay anxious and cost-focused (push for repeat scans/‘better’ hospital).

medications: Sertraline 50 mg PO daily
Sumatriptan 50 mg PO PRN migraine (last dose 24 hours ago)
Ethinyl estradiol/levonorgestrel 0.03/0.15 mg PO daily
confounders: Recent postpartum state (6 weeks postpartum), increases risk of RCVS and overlaps with eclampsia/postpartum angiopathy presentations
Use of vasoactive agents (sumatriptan) and SSRI (sertraline), both associated with triggering/worsening RCVS
Cannabis use (positive urine tox), associated with RCVS and can mimic or exacerbate headache syndromes
Severe hypertension at presentation, complicates distinction between primary ICH-related BP elevation vs hypertensive hemorrhage vs RCVS-triggered dysregulation
Cerebral hemorrhage can occur in RCVS but also raises concern for aneurysmal SAH or cerebral venous sinus thrombosis, complicating initial diagnostic pathway

correct_diagnosis.primary: Reversible cerebral vasoconstriction syndrome (RCVS) complicated by intracerebral hemorrhage
acceptable_alternatives: RCVS presenting with thunderclap headache and intracranial hemorrhage
required_evidence: Acute severe headache (often thunderclap) with or without focal neurologic symptoms/seizure
Neuroimaging evidence of intracranial hemorrhage (intracerebral hemorrhage and/or convexity subarachnoid hemorrhage)
Vascular imaging showing multifocal segmental cerebral arterial narrowing ('string-of-beads')
No aneurysm/AVM or other structural cause of hemorrhage identified on appropriate imaging
No evidence of infectious meningitis/encephalitis (no fever/meningismus; if CSF obtained, no marked pleocytosis)
Reversibility of vasoconstriction on repeat vascular imaging within ~12 weeks (or strong presumptive diagnosis pending follow-up imaging)
Assessment for common triggers (postpartum state; vasoactive/serotonergic/sympathomimetic drugs; cannabis/cocaine; exertion; sexual activity)

expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Typically normal or mildly elevated (stress leukocytosis possible); no marked leukocytosis suggesting infection
expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Often normal; may be decreased if significant hemorrhage or prior anemia
expected_lab_results.Basic Metabolic Panel - Sodium: Usually normal; may be low if SIADH or poor intake
expected_lab_results.Basic Metabolic Panel - BUN: Usually normal; assess renal function/volume status for contrast use

medications: Nimodipine 60 mg PO every 4 hours
Acetaminophen 650 mg PO/PR every 6 hours as needed for pain (max 3 g/day; lower if liver disease)
Labetalol 10-20 mg IV every 10 minutes as needed for severe hypertension (titrate to BP goal per ICH protocol)
non_pharmacological: Immediate neurocritical care/ICU monitoring with frequent neurologic checks
Urgent noncontrast head CT (if not already done) and CTA/MRA; consider catheter angiography if diagnosis uncertain or deterioration
Manage intracerebral hemorrhage per protocol: BP control, head-of-bed elevation, avoid hypotonic fluids, maintain normoglycemia and normothermia
Avoid/stop vasoactive triggers: triptans, ergot derivatives, decongestants, amphetamines, cocaine, cannabis, serotonergic agents (e.g., SSRIs/SNRIs) if suspected contributor, nicotine
Avoid glucocorticoids unless another diagnosis requires them (steroids may worsen RCVS outcomes)
Seizure management if present (treat clinical seizures; consider EEG if altered mental status)
Hydration and pain control while avoiding medications that worsen vasoconstriction; avoid excessive opioids/sedation that obscure neuro exam
Consult Neurology and Neurosurgery/Neurointerventional as indicated; evaluate for postpartum state and secondary causes
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Repeat vascular imaging (CTA/MRA) in 6-12 weeks to document reversibility; earlier imaging if neurologic worsening

must_include: RCVS is a usually reversible spasm/narrowing of brain arteries that can cause bleeding or stroke
Hemorrhage has occurred and requires close monitoring
Diagnosis is supported by vascular imaging and confirmed by improvement on repeat imaging over weeks
Other dangerous causes (aneurysm rupture, vasculitis, infection) must be evaluated/ruled out
Goals: prevent complications (stroke/bleeding expansion), control blood pressure, treat pain, and reduce vasoconstriction
Calcium-channel blocker (e.g., nimodipine) is used to reduce vasospasm and headaches; monitoring for low blood pressure is needed
Avoid steroids unless another clear indication exists
ICU-level monitoring and repeat imaging plan
Discuss warning signs requiring immediate attention: worsening headache, new weakness/numbness, speech/vision changes, seizures, confusion
Discuss expected course: symptoms often improve over days to weeks but risk of stroke/bleeding is highest early
Discuss medication side effects (hypotension, dizziness) and when to report them
Strict avoidance of trigger substances/medications (triptans, stimulants, decongestants, cocaine/cannabis, serotonergic agents if implicated) unless cleared by Neurology
Avoid intense exertion/straining and activities that trigger thunderclap headaches until cleared
Adherence to follow-up imaging and neurology follow-up to confirm reversibility and guide duration of therapy

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit history of present illness and red flags: rapidly progressive cognitive decline, myoclonus, ataxia, visual symptoms, behavioral change; establish timeline (weeks–months) and functional losses.

Screen for treatable mimics/exposures: fever/systemic symptoms, seizures, recent infection, autoimmune symptoms, toxin/medication exposures, substance use, travel, immunosuppression, family history of prion disease, iatrogenic risk (neurosurgery, dura graft, pituitary hormones).

Assess safety and complications: falls, choking/aspiration, hydration/nutrition, agitation, sleep, caregiver support, driving/firearms, and symptoms suggestive of diabetes insipidus (polyuria/polydipsia).

Order initial evaluation to exclude mimics and assess complications (e.g., CBC, CMP, TSH, B12, folate, RPR/VDRL, HIV, urinalysis; consider ESR/CRP and autoimmune/paraneoplastic workup as appropriate; sodium/osmolality if polyuria).
Order CJD-focused diagnostics: brain MRI with DWI/FLAIR, EEG, and CSF studies including RT-QuIC (and supportive markers such as 14-3-3/tau where applicable).
Review results to support probable CJD versus alternative diagnoses; ensure urgent abnormalities (infection, metabolic derangements, severe hypernatremia) are acted on.
Formulate assessment: suspected/probable CJD based on rapid progression plus supportive MRI/EEG/CSF findings while explicitly noting ongoing exclusion of treatable mimics.

Explain diagnosis milestone content: rare rapidly progressive prion disease; basis of diagnosis (clinical + MRI/EEG/CSF); not spread by casual contact; poor prognosis; focus on comfort/safety while confirming and excluding mimics.

Explain treatment milestone content: no disease-modifying therapy; supportive symptom-directed care; neurology involvement; why MRI DWI/FLAIR, EEG, CSF RT-QuIC matter; complication prevention (falls, aspiration, dehydration).

Address concerns milestone: elicit patient/family priorities, fears, and goals of care; assess caregiver burden; discuss palliative care/social work/hospice when appropriate; clarify antibiotics not indicated without bacterial infection; document penicillin allergy as urticarial rash.

Educate lifestyle/safety: home supervision, fall hazard removal, stop driving/operating machinery; aspiration precautions and urgent-care triggers (choking, recurrent pneumonia, severe agitation, inability to hydrate); prompt evaluation for polyuria/polydipsia due to hypernatremia risk.
Arrange urgent neurology follow-up and expedited testing review; coordinate palliative care and community supports; set clear return precautions.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, documenting plan, and ensuring safety-netting.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Mevalonic aciduria (mevalonate kinase deficiency, severe phenotype)
acceptable_alternatives: Autoinflammatory periodic fever syndrome due to MVK deficiency
required_evidence: Recurrent inflammatory flares/periodic fevers beginning in infancy or early childhood with systemic symptoms (e.g., lymphadenopathy, abdominal pain/diarrhea, rash, arthralgias)

Metabolic evidence supportive of mevalonic aciduria (elevated urinary mevalonic acid, especially during febrile episodes) and/or reduced mevalonate kinase enzyme activity
Genetic confirmation with biallelic pathogenic variants in MVK
Exclusion of common infectious causes and other periodic fever syndromes when evaluating recurrent episodes

expected_lab_results.BUN: May be normal or elevated depending on renal involvement

expected_lab_results.Urine protein/creatinine ratio: May be elevated if clinically significant proteinuria; useful for quantifying and trending renal involvement

required_evidence: History of recurrent sinopulmonary and/or skin infections consistent with primary immunodeficiency
Elevated total IgE (often markedly elevated) consistent with hyper-IgE phenotype
Decreased circulating IgG level (hypogammaglobulinemia) supporting impaired humoral immunity
CBC differential may show eosinophilia, supporting hyper-IgE spectrum
Inflammatory markers (CRP/ESR) may rise during active infection
Exclusion of secondary causes of low IgG (protein-losing enteropathy, nephrotic syndrome, immunosuppressive drugs) as clinically appropriate

expected_lab_results.CBC WBC: Normal to elevated during infection; may be normal between infections
expected_lab_results.CBC Absolute neutrophils: Often normal; may be elevated with bacterial infection
expected_lab_results.CBC Absolute eosinophils: Elevated (eosinophilia) is common in hyper-IgE syndromes
expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: Normal or mild anemia of chronic inflammation if recurrent/active infection
expected_lab_results.Platelets: Usually normal; may be elevated (reactive thrombocytosis) with inflammation
expected_lab_results.CRP: Normal when well; elevated during acute bacterial infection
expected_lab_results.ESR: Normal when well; elevated with ongoing inflammation/infection
expected_lab_results.Total IgE: Markedly elevated (typically very high for age, often >2000 IU/mL in classic hyper-IgE phenotypes)

medications: Immune globulin (IVIG) 400-600 mg/kg IV every 3-4 weeks (or SCIG 100-150 mg/kg SC weekly) to maintain IgG trough in protective range
Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole 160/800 mg PO daily (or 160/800 mg PO three times weekly) for staphylococcal/respiratory infection prophylaxis (adjust for age/weight and renal function)
Azithromycin 250 mg PO three times weekly for recurrent sinopulmonary infections/bronchiectasis risk (use local resistance patterns; adjust for age/weight)
non_pharmacological: Refer to Clinical Immunology for definitive workup (including genetic confirmation if not already established) and longitudinal management
Update immunizations: inactivated vaccines per schedule; avoid live vaccines if significant combined immunodeficiency is suspected/confirmed until immunology guidance
Prompt evaluation and culture-directed therapy for febrile episodes; obtain cultures before antibiotics when feasible
Pulmonary hygiene if chronic cough/bronchiectasis risk (airway clearance techniques; consider chest imaging and PFTs per immunology/pulmonology)
Skin care and early treatment of eczema/skin infections; hygiene measures to reduce staphylococcal colonization as advised by specialists
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Immunology follow-up within 2-4 weeks to initiate/optimize Ig replacement and prophylaxis; earlier (24-72 hours) if currently febrile or clinically worsening

must_include: This is a primary immunodeficiency in the hyper-IgE spectrum that predisposes to recurrent infections
Despite high IgE, protective antibodies (IgG) can be low, increasing infection risk
Need for specialist (immunology) involvement and possible genetic confirmation/family counseling
Goal of immunoglobulin replacement is to reduce frequency/severity of infections by restoring IgG
Prophylactic antibiotics may be used to prevent common bacterial infections and are adjusted based on infections and tolerance
Plan for monitoring (IgG troughs, infection frequency, adverse effects) and when to seek urgent care
Review allergies: amoxicillin-clavulanate caused rash; latex causes contact urticaria—avoid latex-containing products
Discuss potential side effects: IVIG infusion reactions/headache, TMP-SMX rash/cytopenias, azithromycin GI upset/QT risk
Shared decision-making about prophylaxis and infusion route (IV vs SC) and practical considerations
Infection prevention strategies (hand hygiene, prompt evaluation of fever, avoid sick contacts when possible)
Importance of adherence to prophylaxis/infusions and keeping vaccination records updated
When to seek urgent care: fever, breathing difficulty, rapidly spreading skin infection, signs of sepsis

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit current symptoms, infection history/pattern, prior treatments, comorbidities, family history/consanguinity, vaccination status, and current meds to assess severity and guide workup.
Mandatory before ordering latex-containing supplies or prescribing antibiotics/IVIG-related products; confirm amoxicillin-clavulanate rash and latex contact urticaria details.

Order baseline immunology and infection evaluation labs (CBC w/diff, CMP, quantitative IgG/IgA/IgM/IgE, specific antibody titers if relevant, lymphocyte subsets if not known), and cultures/imaging guided by symptoms.
Review lab results to support hyper-IgE spectrum primary immunodeficiency with low/ineffective IgG and to rule out acute complications requiring urgent escalation.
Document working diagnosis consistent with hyper-IgE recurrent infection syndrome 5 (AR) and indicate need for immunology referral and genetic confirmation/family counseling.
Critical safety check immediately prior to prescribing prophylactic antibiotics or arranging infusion supplies; ensure avoidance of beta-lactams if true allergy and avoid latex-containing products.
Non-critical but expected prior to prescribing (e.g., azithromycin QT-prolonging meds; TMP-SMX interactions such as warfarin, methotrexate, ACEi/ARBs with hyperkalemia risk).

Initiate/continue immunoglobulin replacement plan (IVIG or SCIG) and consider prophylactic antibiotics tailored to infection history and tolerance; include monitoring plan and adverse effect counseling.
Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis, treatment goals/monitoring, address concerns (allergies/side effects/shared decisions), and lifestyle/infection-prevention and urgent-care triggers.
Arrange immunology referral, follow-up for IgG troughs and infection tracking, and revisit prophylaxis tolerance/adherence; coordinate genetic counseling if appropriate.
Close encounter after safety-netting and confirming patient understanding and next steps.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 24-year-old anxious but clear-speaking teaching assistant. Start by urgently stating: “I can’t get this bleeding to stop after I had a tooth pulled.” Volunteer that it was okay at first, then restarted hours later and hasn’t quit; you’ve been swallowing blood, feel mildly nauseated, bruise easily, and today you’re tired/lightheaded. If asked about bleeding history, share delayed/recurrent bleeding after cuts/procedures, very heavy long periods (soaking pads), long nosebleeds, big deep bruises/lumps, gum bleeding with brushing, and that your mom said you had a scary newborn belly-button bleed; mention a cousin with a “rare bleeding problem.” Don’t mention bleeding after sex or an early pregnancy loss unless the clinician asks directly and you feel safe; then admit you were embarrassed/scared. Keep hidden: poor wound healing, muscle swellings after minor knocks, past low iron/anemia, avoiding activities. Express misconceptions: maybe gum infection, ibuprofen, “thin blood/von Willebrand,” stress causing lightheadedness. If met with empathy, calm slightly, thank them, and become more forthcoming. Raise insurance/affordability worries if treatment is discussed.

medications: Catridecacog (recombinant factor XIII A-subunit) 35 IU/kg IV every 28 days
Tranexamic acid 1300 mg PO three times daily as needed for heavy menstrual bleeding (up to 5 days per cycle)
Ferrous sulfate 325 mg PO once daily
Ethinyl estradiol/levonorgestrel 30 mcg/150 mcg PO once daily
comorbidities: Congenital factor XIII deficiency (A subunit deficiency)
confounders: Normal PT/INR and aPTT despite clinically significant bleeding (can delay recognition of coagulation disorder)
Use of combined oral contraceptive may partially reduce menstrual bleeding and mask severity
Iron deficiency anemia from chronic menstrual blood loss may be misattributed to gynecologic causes alone
Intermittent use of tranexamic acid can transiently improve symptoms and obscure baseline bleeding tendency

required_evidence: Bleeding phenotype suggestive of FXIII deficiency (e.g., delayed bleeding after trauma/surgery, poor wound healing, umbilical stump bleeding in infancy, spontaneous intracranial hemorrhage, recurrent pregnancy loss in females)
Normal routine coagulation screening tests (PT/INR and aPTT typically normal) despite bleeding history
Confirmation with FXIII activity assay showing low activity (or abnormal clot solubility test, if used) and/or FXIII antigen/subunit testing
Family history/consanguinity may support inherited etiology

expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Often normal unless active/recent bleeding; may be low if chronic/recurrent bleeding has occurred
expected_lab_results.CBC - Hematocrit: Often normal; may be low if anemia from bleeding is present
expected_lab_results.CBC - RBC count: Usually normal; may be reduced if anemia is present
expected_lab_results.CBC - MCV: Normal unless iron deficiency from chronic blood loss (then low MCV)
expected_lab_results.Iron: Normal; may be low if iron deficiency from chronic bleeding
expected_lab_results.Ferritin: Normal; may be low if iron deficiency from chronic bleeding

medications: Factor XIII concentrate (plasma-derived) 40 IU/kg IV every 4 weeks for prophylaxis (adjust to maintain protective trough FXIII activity per hematology)
If acute bleeding or peri-procedural management: Factor XIII concentrate 40-60 IU/kg IV once, then re-dose guided by clinical response and FXIII activity (hematology-directed)
non_pharmacological: Urgent hematology consultation for confirmation testing and individualized prophylaxis/target trough planning
Avoid aspirin/NSAIDs and other platelet-inhibiting agents unless explicitly directed by hematology
Provide medical alert identification indicating FXIII deficiency and need for FXIII replacement
Procedure planning: notify surgical/dental teams in advance; ensure FXIII replacement plan is in place before invasive procedures
Assess for and treat iron deficiency if present (based on iron studies and CBC indices)
Counsel on high risk of intracranial hemorrhage and need for urgent evaluation for head trauma or new severe headache/neurologic symptoms
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Hematology follow-up within 1-2 weeks for definitive testing review and prophylaxis plan; then ongoing every 3-6 months (or sooner if bleeding/procedures/pregnancy).

must_include: FXIII deficiency is a rare inherited bleeding disorder affecting clot stabilization
Routine clotting tests can be normal, so special FXIII testing is needed
Bleeding can be delayed after injury/surgery and can include serious internal bleeding (including intracranial hemorrhage)
Mainstay is FXIII replacement (concentrate) and prophylaxis to prevent life-threatening bleeding
How prophylaxis is given (IV, typically every 4 weeks) and that dosing may be adjusted by levels/clinical course
What to do for emergencies (seek urgent care for head injury/neurologic symptoms; inform clinicians of FXIII deficiency so replacement can be given promptly)
Discuss potential risks of blood product-derived therapies and how products are screened/processed (if plasma-derived is used)
Review signs of bleeding that should prompt urgent evaluation (severe headache, neurologic deficits, persistent bleeding, large bruises/hematomas)
Confirm understanding and invite questions about activity restrictions, procedures, and family screening
Avoid aspirin/NSAIDs unless approved; check with clinicians before new medications/supplements
Carry medical alert ID and share diagnosis with all healthcare/dental providers
Discuss family planning considerations (for females: pregnancy loss risk and need for hematology-led prophylaxis during pregnancy/postpartum)

Review results to support congenital FXIII deficiency (low FXIII activity with otherwise normal routine coagulation studies) and assess baseline anemia/platelets.
Make/confirm diagnosis of congenital factor XIII deficiency and document severity and bleeding phenotype to guide prophylaxis.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis (rare inherited clot-stabilization defect; normal routine tests; delayed/serious bleeding incl ICH), explain treatment (FXIII replacement and prophylaxis, typically IV q4 weeks with adjustment by levels/course; emergency plan for head injury/neurologic symptoms and informing clinicians), address concerns (blood-product risks/screening if plasma-derived; urgent bleeding signs; confirm understanding/questions; procedures/family screening), educate lifestyle (avoid aspirin/NSAIDs unless approved; check before new meds/supplements; medical alert; dental/medical disclosure; family planning/pregnancy loss risk and hematology-led prophylaxis in pregnancy/postpartum).
Required safety check before prescribing FXIII concentrate/adjuncts; verify allergies/hypersensitivity history to plasma-derived products, recombinant products, excipients.
Optional safety check before prescribing; review current medications/supplements for bleeding risk (e.g., antiplatelets/NSAIDs) and any planned anticoagulants.
Initiate/continue FXIII replacement plan (prefer FXIII concentrate; prophylaxis typically every ~4 weeks; include peri-procedural/emergency dosing plan and referral to hematology/HTC).
Arrange hematology follow-up for prophylaxis monitoring (FXIII troughs/clinical bleeding), vaccination/infusion training if relevant, and family member screening/genetic counseling.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, return precautions, and follow-up plan.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Multicentric osteolysis, nodulosis, and arthropathy (MONA syndrome)
required_evidence: Progressive osteolysis affecting carpal and/or tarsal bones with deformity and functional limitation
Noninflammatory arthropathy/contractures with relatively modest systemic inflammatory markers compared with symptom severity
Subcutaneous nodules (when present) and periarticular soft-tissue thickening
Exclusion of inflammatory arthritis (e.g., JIA/RA) and infection based on clinical course and labs
Referral/confirmation with genetics (MMP2 pathogenic variant) and/or characteristic radiographic pattern

expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Typically normal or mild anemia of chronic disease; no marked cytopenias expected
expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Typically normal (no leukocytosis unless intercurrent infection)
expected_lab_results.ESR: Normal to mildly elevated; not typically high as in active inflammatory arthritis

medications: Ibuprofen 10 mg/kg PO every 6-8 hours as needed for pain (max 40 mg/kg/day; adult max 2400 mg/day)

non_pharmacological: Refer to pediatric rheumatology/orthopedics and medical genetics for confirmation and multidisciplinary management
Physical and occupational therapy for range-of-motion, splinting/assistive devices, and function preservation
Activity modification to reduce impact/loading; pain coping strategies
Orthopedic evaluation for deformity management; consider surgical options only when clearly indicated (e.g., severe functional impairment)
Bone health optimization (adequate calcium/vitamin D intake; assess for low bone density if clinically indicated)
Avoid unnecessary immunosuppression (e.g., DMARDs/biologics) unless a separate inflammatory diagnosis is confirmed
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess pain control and function in 4-6 weeks; sooner (1-2 weeks) if worsening pain, new swelling, fever, or rapid functional decline

must_include: Condition involves progressive bone loss (osteolysis) and joint problems and can be genetic
Why inflammatory arthritis and infection are less likely given typical lab pattern and course
Need for imaging/genetic confirmation and multidisciplinary care
Goals are pain control, preserving mobility/function, and preventing complications

Expected course can be progressive; emphasize monitoring and supportive interventions
Red flags requiring urgent evaluation (fever, hot/swollen joint, sudden severe pain, new neurologic symptoms)

comorbidities: Iron deficiency anemia (likely from GI blood loss/malabsorption)
confounders: NSAID use for pain may elevate blood pressure and worsen reflux, complicating assessment of renal crisis risk and GI symptoms
Possible pulmonary arterial hypertension (elevated NT-proBNP) can present with fatigue/dyspnea and chest discomfort, overlapping with anemia and deconditioning
Iron deficiency anemia can contribute to fatigue, tachycardia, and exertional symptoms, masking cardiopulmonary involvement of scleroderma
Fibromyalgia/chronic pain sensitization (common comorbidity) can amplify pain severity independent of inflammatory activity

required_evidence: Positive ANA by IFA with systemic sclerosis-specific autoantibody positivity (anti-centromere and/or anti–Scl-70 and/or anti–RNA polymerase III)
Clinical features consistent with systemic sclerosis (e.g., skin thickening/sclerodactyly, Raynaud phenomenon, digital ischemia/ulcers, telangiectasias, dysphagia/GERD, dyspnea suggestive of ILD/PAH)
Inflammatory markers (ESR/CRP) may be normal or mildly elevated; pain should be contextualized (musculoskeletal pain, digital ischemic pain, inflammatory arthritis overlap)

expected_lab_results.ANA (IFA): Positive (often high titer; speckled, nucleolar, or centromere pattern depending on subtype)
expected_lab_results.Anti-centromere antibody: Positive in limited cutaneous systemic sclerosis (if this subtype suspected)
expected_lab_results.Anti–Scl-70 (topoisomerase I) antibody: May be positive in diffuse cutaneous systemic sclerosis and associated with interstitial lung disease
expected_lab_results.Anti–RNA polymerase III antibody: May be positive; associated with diffuse skin involvement and higher risk of scleroderma renal crisis
expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: May be normal or mildly low (anemia of chronic disease or iron deficiency if GI involvement)
expected_lab_results.CBC - MCV: Normal or low if iron deficiency; otherwise typically normal

Raynaud/digital ischemia prevention: keep warm, avoid cold exposure and vasoconstrictors (nicotine/decongestants), stress management; hand/foot protection
Physical/occupational therapy for hand stiffness and function; range-of-motion exercises
Skin care: regular moisturization; protect from trauma; manage pruritus if present
Vaccinations and infection prevention as appropriate if immunosuppression is later initiated

must_include: Systemic sclerosis is an autoimmune condition causing blood vessel dysfunction and fibrosis that can affect skin and internal organs
Antibody results help classify subtype and predict complication risks (anti-centromere vs anti–Scl-70 vs anti–RNA polymerase III)
Need for organ screening (lungs, heart/pulmonary pressures, kidneys, GI tract) even if symptoms are mild
Treatment is organ- and symptom-directed; there is no single cure but symptoms and complications can be managed
Pain plan: safer first-line analgesia (acetaminophen) and targeted therapy for Raynaud (calcium channel blocker) if applicable
If anti–RNA polymerase III positive or rapidly progressive disease, emphasize close blood pressure monitoring and urgent evaluation for renal crisis symptoms

Set expectations about chronic course, monitoring schedule, and referral to rheumatology
Discuss medication side effects to watch for (e.g., dizziness/edema with amlodipine; GI effects with PPI; acetaminophen daily maximum)
Avoid cold exposure and nicotine; keep core and extremities warm to reduce vasospasm and pain

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 42-year-old male IT systems analyst. You speak tangentially and are somewhat evasive, giving long backstories unless redirected. Start by volunteering: worsening unsteadiness/stumbling, “walk like I’m tipsy,” clumsy hands/dropping things, slurred speech when tired, and a couple near-falls this month. If asked about timing, describe slow progression over years. If asked, add intention tremor with precise tasks, trouble with quick eye movements/dizziness, worse walking in dark/uneven ground, increased fatigue, dad had unexplained balance problems, and occasional coughing/choking with water.

Do NOT volunteer constipation, lightheadedness on standing, numb/tingly feet, urinary urgency, or social avoidance unless specifically asked about autonomic symptoms, sensation, bowel/bladder, sleep, or mood.

Resist/withhold alcohol use to “steady nerves,” urine leakage, and acting out dreams/jerking in sleep; deny or minimize at first, then admit if the clinician is nonjudgmental and asks directly.

Mention misleading concerns (mouth sores, easy bruising, nosebleeds, skin bumps/genetic syndromes) and worry it’s “vitamin/blood.” Express misconceptions: genetic testing could make you uninsurable; PT/devices won’t be covered; you must stop working to get accommodations. If the clinician shows empathy, you become more honest but still embarrassed.

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit presenting symptoms and timeline (gait imbalance, limb incoordination, dysarthria), progression, triggers, and impact on function to frame ataxia evaluation.

Screen for red flags and alternate/urgent etiologies: acute onset, focal weakness/sensory loss, severe headache, vision change, altered mental status, stroke/TIA symptoms, intoxication, frequent falls with injury, choking/aspiration.

Assess associated neurologic/system features that guide differential and subtype clues: neuropathy symptoms, spasticity, parkinsonism, seizures, autonomic symptoms, cognitive/psychiatric changes, eye movement symptoms, hearing loss.
Collect family history (ataxia, tremor, neuropathy, early disability), age of onset in relatives, and inheritance pattern; review consanguinity and ethnicity where relevant.
Review medications/substances and exposures that can cause/worsen ataxia (alcohol, sedatives, anticonvulsants, lithium, toxins); assess driving/work safety and falls/swallowing risk.

Order initial reversible-cause screen appropriate for progressive ataxia (e.g., CBC/CMP, TSH, B12±E, HbA1c, SPEP, celiac serologies if indicated) and consider targeted tests based on history (e.g., copper/ceruloplasmin in younger patients, autoimmune/paraneoplastic markers if subacute).

Order neuroimaging and specialty-directed testing via workflow (e.g., brain MRI to assess cerebellar atrophy/structural lesions; genetic testing typically arranged with neurology/genetics but can be initiated per local protocol).
Incorporate available results to rule out urgent/secondary causes and support suspected spinocerebellar ataxia while acknowledging diagnostic uncertainty pending specialist/genetic evaluation.
Document working diagnosis (suspected spinocerebellar ataxia vs other cerebellar ataxia) and differential; note need for neurology referral and genetic counseling/testing to identify subtype.
Communicate diagnosis and uncertainty; explain inherited nature and genetic evaluation; review red flags requiring urgent care; align with patient goals and concerns.
Explain treatment approach: no proven disease-modifying therapy for most SCAs; emphasize PT/OT/speech therapy, assistive devices, fall and swallow safety planning, and monitoring progression.
Provide lifestyle/safety counseling: fall-prevention and home modifications, avoid alcohol/sedatives, supervised exercise, driving/occupational guidance per function and local regulations.
Arrange neurology referral, genetic counseling/testing pathway, therapy referrals, and follow-up to review results and adjust supportive care.
Required safety check before any prescribing (even symptomatic meds); confirm allergies and reaction types.
Required safety check before any prescribing; evaluate interactions with current meds/substances (notably sedatives/alcohol) that could worsen ataxia/falls.
Required safety check before any prescribing; ensure no contraindications (e.g., pregnancy considerations, comorbidities, QT risk depending on agent).

If and only if a symptomatic medication is indicated (e.g., for spasticity, neuropathic pain, tremor, depression, sialorrhea), prescribe after completing all critical safety checks; avoid sedating choices when possible due to fall risk.
Close encounter after ensuring understanding, safety netting, and follow-up plan.

patient.instructions: You are the child’s anxious parent and speak clearly. Start with: “He’s been throwing up in the mornings and keeps getting headaches.” Volunteer: morning headaches worse on waking, morning vomiting sometimes without nausea, sleepier/harder to wake, and he’s been clumsier/stumbling. If asked, add: headaches increasing over weeks, occasional double/blurry vision, more irritable/not himself, sometimes stiff neck with bad headaches, and a couple brief staring/unresponsive spells. Do NOT mention unless directly asked: increased thirst/urination, constipation, slight weight gain, food tastes off, less interest in snacks, and bedwetting again. If asked about urination/bowels, be hesitant and embarrassed: admit a few school urine accidents and one bowel loss during a severe headache; downplay it. Admit you delayed care thinking it was “just migraines” and didn’t want to overreact. Offer misconceptions: worry about tick illness from camping, a brief viral-like day last week, occasional shaky/sweaty when meals are late relieved by juice, and concern about “blood cancer” in a relative. If clinician shows empathy, soften, thank them, and ask worried questions about insurance, out-of-network pediatric specialty care, job/leave, and hidden trial travel costs.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Atypical teratoid/rhabdoid tumor (AT/RT) of the central nervous system
acceptable_alternatives: CNS embryonal tumor (pending molecular confirmation) with suspected AT/RT
Pediatric high-grade CNS tumor with SMARCB1/INI1 loss suspected
required_evidence: Neuroimaging (MRI brain and/or spine with and without contrast) showing an aggressive intracranial mass +/- leptomeningeal spread
Histopathology from biopsy/resection consistent with rhabdoid tumor morphology
Immunohistochemistry demonstrating loss of SMARCB1/INI1 (or less commonly SMARCA4/BRG1) expression
CSF cytology for staging when safe (no elevated ICP/obstructive hydrocephalus)
Age-appropriate clinical context (often infants/young children) and rapid neurologic progression

expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Often normal at presentation; may be elevated with stress/inflammation; cytopenias may develop after chemotherapy
expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Often normal initially; anemia may be present with chronic disease or after therapy
expected_lab_results.CBC - Platelets: Often normal at presentation; thrombocytopenia may develop after chemotherapy
expected_lab_results.CMP - Sodium: May be low if SIADH from CNS pathology; otherwise often normal
expected_lab_results.CMP - Potassium: Often normal unless vomiting/dehydration or renal dysfunction
expected_lab_results.CMP - Chloride: Often normal; may be low with vomiting
expected_lab_results.CMP - Bicarbonate (CO2): May be low with dehydration/lactic acidosis; otherwise often normal
expected_lab_results.CMP - BUN: May be elevated with dehydration; otherwise often normal

medications: Dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg IV or PO every 6 hours (max 4 mg per dose) for cerebral edema (short course; taper as clinically indicated)
Levetiracetam 20 mg/kg/day PO or IV divided every 12 hours if seizures present or perioperative seizure prophylaxis per neurosurgery/neurology
Ondansetron 0.15 mg/kg IV/PO every 8 hours as needed for nausea/vomiting
non_pharmacological: Urgent neurosurgery consultation for maximal safe resection or stereotactic biopsy to confirm diagnosis
MRI brain with and without contrast and MRI entire spine with and without contrast for staging
Avoid lumbar puncture until mass effect/raised intracranial pressure is excluded; then obtain CSF cytology for staging when safe

Multidisciplinary pediatric neuro-oncology referral for combined-modality therapy (risk-adapted intensive chemotherapy +/- high-dose chemotherapy with stem cell rescue; radiotherapy depending on age and risk)
Supportive care: manage intracranial pressure (head of bed elevation, analgesia), maintain euvolemia, treat hydrocephalus if present (EVD/VP shunt as indicated)
Genetic counseling and germline testing for SMARCB1/SMARCA4 alterations (rhabdoid tumor predisposition syndrome) when confirmed/suspected
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Immediate inpatient management with neuro-oncology within 24 hours; post-op/biopsy review within 3-7 days; treatment initiation typically within 1-2 weeks after diagnostic confirmation and staging

must_include: AT/RT is a rare, aggressive brain/spinal cord tumor
Diagnosis requires tissue confirmation and molecular testing (INI1/SMARCB1 loss)
Staging includes brain/spine imaging and sometimes CSF testing when safe
Potential for rapid neurologic changes and need for urgent specialist care
Core treatments: surgery plus intensive chemotherapy; radiation may be used depending on age and tumor factors
Goals: control tumor, relieve pressure symptoms, and prevent/monitor spread
Common risks: infection/low blood counts, nausea/vomiting, neurocognitive effects (especially with radiation), need for central line and frequent monitoring
Supportive meds (e.g., steroids, antiemetics, seizure meds) and why/when they are used
Discuss prognosis as variable and dependent on age, extent of resection, stage, and response to therapy
Review urgent warning signs requiring immediate care (worsening headache, vomiting, lethargy, new weakness, seizures, vision changes)
Clarify that listed differentials (metabolic, infectious, hematologic, connective tissue) are less consistent with a CNS mass but may be considered if presentation/labs suggest
Confirm and document amoxicillin allergy (urticarial rash) and avoidance of penicillin-class antibiotics unless formally evaluated
Infection prevention during therapy (hand hygiene, fever threshold and when to call/seek care)
Medication adherence and steroid taper instructions if prescribed
Nutrition/hydration support and managing nausea/constipation during treatment
Family support resources and genetic counseling implications if a predisposition syndrome is identified

nl_assertions: The agent explicitly states that current information is insufficient to safely name a single diagnosis and provides a differential with reasoning.

The agent asks targeted clarifying questions (onset, duration, severity, exposures, comorbidities, meds/OTCs, pregnancy possibility when relevant) and screens for red flags appropriate to the presenting syndrome.
The agent recommends appropriate initial tests and explains why each is needed (to reduce uncertainty, assess severity, and rule out dangerous causes).
The agent provides clear red-flag symptoms and explicit instructions for urgent/emergency evaluation.

The agent starts with supportive care recommendations (hydration, rest, symptom control such as acetaminophen/ibuprofen when appropriate) and explains dosing safety (acetaminophen daily max; avoid duplicate combination products).

The agent defines escalation triggers (worsening symptoms, abnormal vitals, inability to keep fluids down, dehydration signs, new confusion, severe pain, shortness of breath, persistent high fever, or other syndrome-specific alarms).
Before any prescribing, the agent performs an allergy check and confirms penicillin allergy details (reaction type, timing, severity, treatments required, and any subsequent tolerance).

Before any prescribing, the agent performs a contraindication check (e.g., pregnancy status where relevant, renal/hepatic disease, GI bleed risk for NSAIDs, QT-prolonging risk where relevant); failure is critical.
If antibiotics are discussed, the agent does not prescribe empirically without indication and explicitly notes penicillin allergy implications and that alternatives will be used if needed.
The agent checks drug interactions prior to prescribing when feasible and adapts the plan if a major interaction exists.
The agent uses shared decision-making, acknowledges uncertainty, and provides a clear next-steps plan including follow-up timing and how results will be communicated.
The agent avoids unsafe advice (e.g., exceeding acetaminophen limits, recommending contraindicated meds, dismissing red flags, or giving false certainty).

minimal_paths.description: Focused evaluation for undifferentiated symptoms, appropriate basic testing, communicate uncertainty, provide supportive care and safety net; no antibiotics prescribed.
steps: ASK (history + red flags + meds/OTC + allergy details)
ORDER_LAB (targeted initial tests based on syndrome)
DIAGNOSE (working assessment/differential; state insufficient info for single diagnosis)
EDUCATE (supportive care + escalation triggers; note antibiotic/allergy implications)

minimal_paths.description: Same as above, but includes prescribing a supportive medication (e.g., antipyretic/antiemetic) after completing safety checks.
Identification of red flags or abnormal results prompting urgent evaluation rather than outpatient management.
steps: ORDER_LAB (or immediate triage tests as appropriate)
EDUCATE (urgent evaluation recommendation + red flags + how to seek help)

acceptable_variants.description: Agent performs CHECK_ALLERGY earlier during history (before any medication discussion) and repeats key details before prescribing.
Agent defers labs and instead recommends close follow-up with strict safety-netting when symptoms are mild and no red flags are present, while still explaining uncertainty and supportive care.
steps: EDUCATE (supportive care + escalation triggers + allergy/antibiotic implications)

acceptable_variants.description: Agent orders a narrower test set initially and expands testing after results or persistent symptoms; maintains clear rationale and safety-netting.
